stories.title: Evian R1 — Aki Iwai Leads at -8; Korda Opens +3, Career Grand Slam Chase in Trouble

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text: Nelly Korda opened the Evian Championship at +3 (74). She is 11 shots off the lead after Round 1. Her career Grand Slam bid — which needed a win here — is in serious trouble. #Evian #LPGA

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text: Aki Iwai fired an 8-under 63 to lead the Amundi Evian Championship after Round 1. The 24-year-old Japanese star leads by 3 shots. Haeran Ryu is second at -5. Korda is at +3, 11 shots back. #Evian #LPGA

facebook.long_form:
The career Grand Slam just got harder.

Nelly Korda opened her Amundi Evian Championship campaign with a +3 (74) on Thursday, leaving her 11 shots off the lead after Round 1. It was the kind of round that erases momentum before a tournament has really started — a bogey on 18, a double bogey on 1, and two more drops on 4 and 5 that turned an early birdie into damage control.

At the top of the leaderboard, Aki Iwai did something remarkable. The 24-year-old Japanese player, ranked World No. 24, fired an 8-under 63 to take the solo lead by three shots. It is the kind of first round that can define a major championship — or evaporate in the French Alps heat over the next three days.

Second place after Round 1 belongs to Haeran Ryu at -5 (66). That storyline writes itself. Ryu won the KPMG Women's PGA Championship earlier this year, ending Korda's calendar Grand Slam bid. She is now three shots behind the leader while Korda is eleven back.

To complete her career Grand Slam, Korda needs Evian and the AIG Women's British Open. She won the Chevron Championship and the US Women's Open in 2026. At +3 after Round 1, she would need one of the great closing runs in major championship history. The Evian is not over. But the margin is brutal.
facebook.image_caption: Korda opened at +3. Iwai leads at -8. Ryu is at -5. The Evian leaderboard after Round 1 is exactly the wrong thing for Korda's career Grand Slam.

article.headline: Korda Opens +3 at Evian: Career Grand Slam Chase in Serious Trouble After Round 1

stories.title: Jon Rahm Returns to Co-Sanctioned Golf at Scottish Open — First Start Since LIV, Ryder Cup Path Reopened

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text: Jon Rahm is playing the Genesis Scottish Open today — his first co-sanctioned PGA Tour/DP World Tour start since joining LIV Golf. He paid the outstanding fines. He signed the agreement. He's back. #ScottishOpen #LIV #Golf

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text: Rory McIlroy, Chris Gotterup (the defending champion), and Robert MacIntyre in the same group at the Scottish Open. Gotterup won the John Deere Classic on Sunday. He's 3 wins deep in 2026 and back at a course he owns. #ScottishOpen

facebook.long_form:
Jon Rahm teed it up at the Genesis Scottish Open on Thursday morning. For any other player in the field, that sentence would be unremarkable. For Rahm, it is the end of a two-year absence from co-sanctioned golf and the beginning of something that could reshape his standing in the sport.

Rahm joined LIV Golf in January 2024. The DP World Tour suspended him, then opened a path for reinstatement. In the agreement that allowed his return to the Scottish Open, Rahm paid all outstanding fines accumulated since his LIV move and committed to playing a specified number of DP World Tour events in the remainder of 2026. This week counts as his fifth event under that arrangement, officially designating him a member in good standing.

The Ryder Cup implication is immediate. Under the membership terms, Rahm is now eligible to earn Race to Dubai points and, if he maintains good standing through 2026 and 2027, he is eligible for European Ryder Cup consideration. Rahm captained Europe to victory at the 2023 Ryder Cup. His presence in future European sides has been a question without an answer for two years.

He called the Scottish Open "one of my favourite weeks." His tee time Thursday was 9:05 a.m. ET, while McIlroy, Gotterup, and MacIntyre — the group everyone is watching — went out earlier at 3:28 a.m. ET.

Rahm is back. What happens next at the Scottish Open and beyond is the story to follow.
facebook.image_caption: Jon Rahm teed off at the Scottish Open today — his first co-sanctioned PGA Tour/DP World Tour start since LIV. He paid the fines. He signed the deal. His Ryder Cup path is open again.

article.headline: Rahm Paid the Fines. Now He's Back. And His Ryder Cup Path Is Open Again.

stories.title: LIV Golf $400M Crisis Deepens — PIF Preparing to Exit, Two Events at Risk

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text: PIF committed $600M to fund LIV Golf through 2026. Delivered roughly $200M so far. The $400M shortfall has LIV Golf Indianapolis and Michigan at risk. PIF already announced it ends support after 2026. #LIVGolf #Golf

facebook.long_form:
The financial reality of LIV Golf is becoming difficult to ignore.

Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund — the entity that has bankrolled LIV Golf since its 2022 launch at a cost of more than $5 billion — announced in April 2026 that it would fund the league only through the remainder of this season. The language of the statement was direct: "The substantial investment required by LIV Golf over a longer term is no longer consistent with the current phase of PIF's investment strategy."

That was April. Now it is July, and the numbers are getting harder to manage. PIF committed approximately $600 million to fund the remaining 2026 events. It has delivered around $200 million — roughly $66 million in May and about $130 million in June. The $400 million that has not arrived is the reason LIV Golf Indianapolis and LIV Golf Team Championship Michigan are now described as events that may not happen. The UK event and Trump National Bedminster are expected to proceed. New Orleans was already postponed earlier this year.

Discussions with potential outside investors are not producing results. Prospective backers want clarity on what LIV Golf looks like in 2027 and which players would stay if PIF steps away. Those are questions that do not have answers yet.

LIV Golf's 2026 schedule may not finish. The league that spent four years trying to rewrite professional golf's economics is now in a position where it may not make it to the end of its own calendar.
facebook.image_caption: PIF committed $600M for the rest of 2026. Delivered roughly $200M. The $400M gap has two LIV events at risk. PIF announced in April it ends support after this season.

article.headline: LIV Golf's $400M Problem: PIF Is Leaving and Two Events Are at Risk

stories.title: Open Championship Last-Chance Qualifier — Potgieter, Bryan, Schmid Among 12 Chasing Final Royal Birkdale Spot

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text: July 13. Royal Birkdale. 12 players. One spot in The Open Championship. Aldrich Potgieter (LIV, World No. 77), Wesley Bryan (missed Final Qualifying by 1 shot), Matti Schmid (T4 PGA Championship) all in. #TheOpen #Golf

facebook.long_form:
On Monday morning, July 13, twelve golfers will arrive at Royal Birkdale for eighteen holes with one thing on the line: a single spot in The Open Championship, which begins on the same course three days later.

The format is new. The R&A created the Open Championship Last-Chance Qualifier for the first time this year, offering one final path into the field at the 154th Open. Twelve players. One round. One ticket.

The names make it worth watching. Aldrich Potgieter is ranked World No. 77, won The Amateur Championship as a 17-year-old in 2022, and has won on the PGA Tour. He is on the LIV Golf roster and earned his LCQ place through the Official World Golf Rankings. Wesley Bryan — the PGA Tour winner who, alongside his brother George, built one of the most-followed YouTube channels in golf — missed Final Qualifying at West Lancashire by a single shot, bogeying the final hole to fall short by one stroke. He has been talking about getting to The Open Championship for years. This is his last chance.

Matti Schmid tied for fourth at the 2026 PGA Championship in May. He was in contention at a major two months ago and is chasing another chance at one.

Eighteen holes. Royal Birkdale. July 13. One of these twelve players gets to come back on Thursday for the first round of The Open.
facebook.image_caption: 12 players. 18 holes. 1 spot at The Open Championship. Monday, July 13, Royal Birkdale. Potgieter, Bryan, Schmid. Someone is getting in.

article.headline: 12 Players, One Spot: Inside the Open Championship's Inaugural Last-Chance Qualifier

stories.title: 2026 PGA Tour Player of the Year Race — Gotterup Makes His Move at Scottish Open

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: Gotterup: 3 wins in 2026. Fitzpatrick: 3 wins. Clark: US Open. Scheffler: World No. 1, 1 win. The PGA Tour POTY race is legitimately open for the first time in years. Scottish Open could shift the balance. #PGATour #Golf

facebook.long_form:
There has not been a genuine multi-player PGA Tour Player of the Year race in several years. 2026 is different.

Chris Gotterup arrived at the Genesis Scottish Open on the back of his third PGA Tour win of the season — the John Deere Classic, completed July 6. He is the defending Scottish Open champion. He is ranked World No. 4. Three wins in a calendar year, on a variety of courses and in different formats, is a substantial argument.

Matt Fitzpatrick also has three wins in 2026. He has been consistent across the globe and has built a case that mirrors Gotterup's in win total while bringing a wider international portfolio of events.

Wyndham Clark won the US Open. A major title weighs heavily in POTY voting — often more heavily than an equivalent number of regular wins. Clark's US Open triumph, combined with his Byron Nelson victory, gives him a credible two-item portfolio with one of those items being the highest-weighted possible result.

Then there is Scottie Scheffler. World No. 1 in the Official World Golf Ranking. One PGA Tour win in 2026 — the Masters — but the argument that sustained dominance across the full season matters as much as wins. POTY voters have given weight to that argument before.

The Scottish Open is the last meaningful tournament before The Open Championship. Four players with genuine cases. Four chances to add to the argument. The vote does not get cast until the season ends, but the campaign starts now.
facebook.image_caption: Gotterup: 3 wins. Fitzpatrick: 3 wins. Clark: US Open. Scheffler: World No. 1. The 2026 PGA Tour POTY race is legitimately open. Scottish Open could be the tipping point.

article.headline: The PGA Tour POTY Race in 2026: Why Scheffler's Crown Is Being Challenged

research.facts:
2026 PGA Tour Player of the Year Race — Gotterup Makes His Move
**Classification:** FOLLOW UP (POTY Race covered July 8)
**New development:** Gotterup won John Deere Classic July 6 for his 3rd win; now leads POTY race discussion heading into Scottish Open.
**Sources:**
- Golf Channel: 2026 POTY race coverage (July 7-8)
- PGA Tour official standings
**Key facts:**
- Gotterup: 3 wins in 2026 (includes John Deere Classic July 6); currently World No. 4; defending Scottish Open champion; arrived at Renaissance Club with enormous momentum
- Chris Gotterup's 3 wins in 2026: John Deere Classic (July 6), + 2 earlier wins (exact events TBD)
- Matt Fitzpatrick: 3 wins in 2026, World No. 6
- Wyndham Clark: won US Open + Byron Nelson in 2026; World No. 8
- Scottie Scheffler: 1 win in 2026 (Masters); World No. 1; in the Scottish Open field
- 2026 context: Scheffler's normal iron grip on POTY disrupted — Gotterup and Fitzpatrick each matching or exceeding his win total
- Scottish Open (July 9-12) opportunity: Gotterup could extend to 4 wins at an event he won last year; Scheffler, Fitzpatrick, and others in field
- POTY voters typically weight majors heavily; Clark's US Open win gives him a strong case despite fewer total wins
